record.field: Logto is an open-source identity and access management platform that adds production-ready authentication and authorization to apps without building it from scratch.

Logto is an open-source identity and access management platform that adds production-ready authentication and authorization to apps without building it from scratch.

Built on OIDC and OAuth 2.1, Logto provides pre-built sign-in flows, customizable UIs and SDKs for 30+ frameworks, so developers can ship secure auth quickly. It targets SaaS, multi-tenant products and AI/agent applications, and positions itself as a modern, self-hostable alternative to Auth0 and Okta. Core capabilities such as SSO, RBAC and organizations are available with no paywall on the self-hosted edition.

Key features
  • OIDC and OAuth 2.1 provider with SAML and enterprise SSO support
  • Role-based access control (RBAC), organizations and native multi-tenancy
  • Social login, passwordless and multi-factor authentication (MFA)
  • Pre-built, customizable sign-in experience and management console
  • SDKs for 30+ frameworks plus a management API
  • Audit logs, brute-force protection and multi-tenant isolation
Best for

Development teams and SaaS vendors that need a self-hosted, standards-based auth layer with multi-tenancy, SSO and RBAC, including builders of AI and agent-based applications who want full control over customer identity.

services: app: depends_on: postgres: condition: service_healthy image: ghcr.io/logto-io/logto:1.27.0 entrypoint: - sh - -c - npm run cli db seed -- --swe && npm start ports: - '{{ PORT_HTTP }}:3001' environment: TRUST_PROXY_HEADER: 1 DB_URL: postgres://logto:{{ logto_postgres_password }}@postgres:5432/logto ENDPOINT: {{ logto_endpoint }} ADMIN_ENDPOINT: {{ logto_admin_endpoint }} volumes: - ./logto_connectors:/etc/logto/packages/core/connectors container_name: '{{ INSTANCE_CODE }}' restart: unless-stopped networks: - internal - agz-proxy-network postgres: image: postgres:17-alpine user: postgres environment: POSTGRES_USER: logto POSTGRES_PASSWORD: {{ logto_postgres_password }} volumes: - ./postgres_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data healthcheck: test: - CMD-SHELL - pg_isready interval: 10s timeout: 5s retries: 5 container_name: '{{ INSTANCE_CODE }}_postgres' restart: unless-stopped networks: - internal networks: internal: driver: bridge agz-proxy-network: external: true

#!/bin/bash set -euo pipefail cd "$SERVICE_PATH" # Dump consistente de PostgreSQL (servicio en marcha) → $BACKUP_DATA_DIR docker compose exec -T postgres sh -c 'pg_dump -U "$POSTGRES_USER" -d "$POSTGRES_DB" --no-owner -Fc' \ > "$BACKUP_DATA_DIR/db.dump" echo "pg_dump OK ($(wc -c < "$BACKUP_DATA_DIR/db.dump") bytes)"

#!/bin/bash set -euo pipefail cd "$SERVICE_PATH" # Levanta solo la DB, espera readiness y restaura docker compose up -d postgres for i in $(seq 1 30); do docker compose exec -T postgres sh -c 'pg_isready -U "$POSTGRES_USER"' && break sleep 2 done docker compose exec -T postgres sh -c 'pg_restore -U "$POSTGRES_USER" -d "$POSTGRES_DB" --clean --if-exists --no-owner' \ < "$RESTORE_DATA_DIR/db.dump" # Verificación: la DB no quedó vacía (sh -c con dobles comillas; $VAR del contenedor con \$) N=$(docker compose exec -T postgres sh -c "psql -U \$POSTGRES_USER -d \$POSTGRES_DB -tAc \"SELECT count(*) FROM information_schema.tables WHERE table_schema='public'\"") echo "tablas restauradas: $N" test "$N" -gt 0
